critic_raw:
This is a well-designed methodology. The chronological split with campaign-level deduplication addresses the two most common sources of leakage in phishing detection benchmarks. Point-in-time snapshotting of reputation features is an explicit acknowledgment of a subtle but real leakage vector. The label construction is appropriately conservative — excluding ambiguous/unresolved cases is the right call even though it reduces dataset size, because training on noisy positives degrades calibration in ways that PR-AUC will expose. The baseline is genuinely strong and tuning parity is explicitly required. Let me look for real issues.

**Issue 1: The analyst disposition label is not receipt-time-available, which creates a selection bias problem for the negative class.**

The claim is that 'confirmed benign' negatives are a clean representation of legitimate enterprise mail. The failure mechanism: analyst disposition is only generated for emails that were *flagged or sampled for review*, not for all inbound mail. The vast majority of traffic that flows through a mail gateway and is never queued for review has no disposition. 'Explicitly resolved as benign' at enterprise scale almost certainly means: emails that were flagged by some prior system and then cleared, or emails sampled by an existing policy. This is not a random or representative sample of the benign class — it is benign mail that looks suspicious enough to have been touched by a triage process. The practical consequence is that the negative training distribution is biased toward edge-case-looking legitimate mail (IT security alerts, bulk newsletter-style internal comms, unusual sender domains). If the model learns to separate 'benign mail that looked suspicious to the previous system' from 'confirmed phish', it may perform well on the holdout (which will have the same selection bias) but the true operational false positive rate on arbitrary inbound mail could be very different. This is not a nit — it is a structural property of how enterprise email disposition data is generated.

Evidence for or against: Check what fraction of the benign class came from flagged-then-cleared workflows vs. random sampling pipelines. If >80% of negatives originated from a queue that was already filtered by a predecessor rule set, the selection bias is material. The ablation on reputation strata will partially surface this: if performance drops sharply on the lowest-reputation benign senders (who are least likely to have been reviewed), that is a signal.

**Issue 2: Near-duplicate clustering does not prevent feature-level leakage from shared infrastructure across campaigns.**

The claim is that cluster-level assignment prevents template reuse from inflating test performance. The mechanism this addresses is: identical or near-identical message content appearing in both splits. What it does not address is shared sending infrastructure — the same botnet IP ranges, compromised domains, lookalike domains, or URL redirect chains used across distinct campaigns. If infrastructure features (sender reputation buckets, routing metadata, URL registrar patterns) are correlated across campaigns that are textually distinct, then training on campaign A teaches the model that IP block X or registrar Y is high-risk, and that signal transfers to campaign B in test — even though campaign B's content was never seen. This is a weaker form of leakage (it does not directly inflate generalization to novel attackers) but it means the 'unseen sender domains' and 'unseen URL domains' subgroup ablations are the right diagnostic. If performance on those subgroups collapses relative to overall PR-AUC, the model is infrastructure-dependent and the headline number is optimistic for novel campaigns. The methodology actually proposes this ablation, which partially redeems the approach — but it should be explicit that this ablation is designed to probe exactly this failure mode, and the threshold for 'acceptable degradation' on unseen domains should be pre-specified rather than post-hoc.

Evidence for or against: Compare PR-AUC on the full test set against PR-AUC restricted to emails whose sender domain AND primary URL domain both appear for the first time in test. If the gap is large (e.g., >10 PR-AUC points), the model is heavily dependent on infrastructure reputation rather than content understanding, and the transformer's advantage over the GBDT may vanish on truly novel campaigns.

**Issue 3: The transformer's advantage may be confounded with tokenization coverage over adversarial text manipulation.**

The claim is that the multimodal transformer provides better representation power than engineered features. A prerequisite for the transformer's mechanism to operate as hypothesized is that its text encoder actually ingests the rendered, decoded message content — not the raw MIME encoding. Phishing emails routinely use HTML entities, zero-width characters, homoglyph substitution, mixed RTL/LTR unicode, base64-encoded HTML bodies, and multi-part MIME structures with text/plain decoys and text/html payloads that differ. If the preprocessing pipeline feeds raw MIME or pre-tokenized text without a rendering/decoding step, the transformer may be operating on content that is systematically different from what an analyst (or recipient) sees. A GBDT with explicit heuristic features for obfuscation (character entropy, unicode category distributions, ratio of HTML entities to text length) might actually be more robust to this than a transformer whose subword tokenizer fragments homoglyphs and unicode escapes unpredictably. The methodology does not specify whether the body representation is rendered HTML, raw HTML, or MIME-decoded plaintext, and this choice materially affects what signal the transformer can access.

Evidence for or against: Add a rendering pipeline and compare model performance on a held-out subset of confirmed obfuscated phish (analyst-tagged or identified by decoding heuristics). If transformer performance drops more than GBDT performance on obfuscated samples relative to straightforward phish, the representation is not capturing the adversarial content manipulation that is the main reason to prefer a text encoder over heuristic features.

**Summary:**
Issue 1 is the most structurally significant — it affects the validity of the negative class and therefore the validity of the PR-AUC number as a proxy for operational false positive rate. Issues 2 and 3 are real but partially mitigated by the proposed subgroup ablations. The methodology is unusually careful for this domain, but Issue 1 is a root-cause problem that no amount of post-hoc ablation fully resolves — it requires knowing the provenance of the negative labels.
all_issues_raised: Confirmed-benign negatives are likely selected from flagged-then-cleared workflows rather than representative inbound mail, creating a selection bias in the negative class that inflates PR-AUC relative to true operational false positive rates.

Shingle-based near-duplicate clustering prevents content-level leakage but not infrastructure-level leakage (shared IPs, registrars, redirect chains across textually distinct campaigns), and the degradation threshold on the unseen-domain subgroup ablation should be pre-specified rather than interpreted post-hoc.

The transformer's text encoding advantage is conditional on receiving rendered/decoded message content; unspecified MIME handling may cause the transformer to operate on adversarially obfuscated text less reliably than engineered obfuscation-aware heuristic features.
all_issues_adjudicated: Confirmed-benign negatives are likely selected from flagged-then-cleared workflows rather than representative inbound mail, creating a selection bias in the negative class that inflates PR-AUC relative to true operational false positive rates.

Shingle-based near-duplicate clustering prevents content-level leakage but not infrastructure-level leakage (shared IPs, registrars, redirect chains across textually distinct campaigns), and the degradation threshold on the unseen-domain subgroup ablation should be pre-specified rather than interpreted post-hoc.

The transformer's text encoding advantage is conditional on receiving rendered/decoded message content; unspecified MIME handling may cause the transformer to operate on adversarially obfuscated text less reliably than engineered obfuscation-aware heuristic features.
